articles.description: On 1 July, an Airbus A330 lifts off from Harare for London under the

articles.title: Zimbabwe's tobacco anchor in Britain holds at £129m
articles.description: Of every £100 of goods Zimbabwe sells to Britain, £96 is tobacco or beverages. While the wider UK–Zimbabwe trade relationship softened in the year to December 2025, Zimbabwean tobacco held its grip, and the IMF projects Zimbabwean GDP growth of 7.5% in 2025 and 5.0% in 2026. The product that built Zimbabwe's name as an exporter is still doing the work.

articles.title: The Apple of haircare gets its German cheque
articles.description: Ruka, the London-based textured-hair company founded by Zimbabwean entrepreneur Varaidzo Tendai Moyo and her co-founder Ugorji Agbai, has closed a $4.5m round co-led by Henkel Ventures and Freedom Trail Capital. The deal, which takes lifetime funding to roughly $10m and brings one of the world's largest consumer-goods groups onto the cap table, is the most meaningful endorsement yet of a Zimbabwean diaspora-founded brand.

more.description: Days before its London return, Air Zimbabwe has switched on a new

more.title: Pardon Mapondera: gone but not forgotten at Venice
more.description: The fifth and final piece in our series on Zimbabwe's pavilion at the 61st Venice Biennale. Pardon Mapondera, the artist whose practice the room finally arranges itself around, treats discarded plastic and glass jars not as ecological signal but as ritual matter, and produces, in the process, the most quietly devotional installation in the Biennale this year.

more.title: Gideon Gomo: from stone to bronze, the ancestral algorithm at Venice
more.description: The fourth in a five-part series on Zimbabwe's pavilion at the 61st Venice Biennale. Gideon Gomo's first sustained body of bronze work, paired with discarded vehicle number plates and Zimbabwean springstone, is the move of a sculptor at full maturity rethinking the material vocabulary of his country's most internationally legible art form.

more.title: Shumba Maasai in Venice
more.description: At the 61st Biennale, the Zimbabwean artist's day-long procession through the city's campi, performed under the borrowed authority of a Murehwa chief, restages the social-sculpture lineage of Joseph Beuys for a contemporary African discursive practice.

more.title: Franklyn Dzingai: family albums and the neural commons at Venice
more.description: The third in a five-part series on Zimbabwe's pavilion at the 61st Venice Biennale. Franklyn Dzingai's reductive cardboard prints, built from the family album, the magazine clipping, the back-room studio photographs of mid-century Zimbabwe, turn out, on close looking, to be the most precisely engineered work in the room.

more.title: Felix Shumba: charcoal and the practice of disappearance at Venice
more.description: The second in a five-part series on Zimbabwe's pavilion at the 61st Venice Biennale. Felix Shumba's six-metre charcoal wall and his Telephone Reader series anchor a body of work that argues drawing is not the act of capturing the seen, but the act of staying present to what is on its way out of sight.

more.title: UK services share in Zimbabwe rises to 8.2%
more.description: The headline UK services trade with Zimbabwe softened year on year. The structural number underneath it tells the better story: Britain's share of all services Zimbabwe imports rose 2.4 percentage points to 8.2%, the highest measured level in years. As Zimbabwean buyers re-rate their global supplier mix, the UK is taking a bigger slice of the country's services demand.

more.title: Zimbabwe's demand lifts British exports by 14.6%
more.description: The Zimbabwean economy is in its strongest stretch in years: 7.5% GDP growth in 2025, a projected 5% in 2026, and that growth is pulling demand for cars, commercial vehicles, medicines and industrial machinery in with it. UK goods exports to Zimbabwe rose 14.6% on the year, the strongest line in an otherwise soft bilateral picture for Britain.

It is a cenotaph marking the exact crash site where the [...] The post Tongogara Monument set for commissioning appeared first on NewZimbabwe.com .

more.title: IN PICTURES -Stakeholders urged to leverage Zimbabwe’s global tourism accolades
more.description: Nqobile Bhebhe nqobile.bhebhe@chronicle.co.zw TOURISM stakeholders have been urged to take pride in the growing list of international accolades that Zimbabwe continues to earn, saying the honours have elevated the country's profile on the global tourism stage and should be aggressively leveraged to market the destination. [caption id=attachment_5396221 align=alignleft width=680] Stakeholders urged to leverage Zimbabwe's global tourism accolades[/caption] Permanent Secretary in the Ministry of Tourism and Hospitality Industry, Dr Takaruza Munyanyiwa, made the call during an oversubscribed consultation meeting on the National Tourism Sector Strategy (NTSS) 2026-2030 in Bulawayo on Tuesday. They are yours, be proud of the accolades. Sometimes I feel that as stakeholders we are in the terraces. This is our thing, we must run with it in your marketing and strategies, said Dr Munyanyiwa. Let's see that hunger to ride on these accolades. Let us harness our collective wisdom, resources, and energy to build our sector. Together, we will shape a vibrant, resilient, and globally competitive tourism sector that uplifts communities, strengthens our economy and reaffirms Zimbabwe's place on the world tourism map. His remarks come as Zimbabwe continues to receive prestigious international recognition, with the accolades placing the country firmly on the global tourism map and enhancing its visibility among international travellers and investors. Over the past year, Zimbabwe won the Gold Award in the Internet and Media Category at the prestigious 2026 Chinese Tourist Welcome (CTW) Awards, recognising the country's innovative destination marketing initiatives targeting the Chinese market. Zimbabwe also strengthened its international standing after being recognised by Forbes Magazine as the Best Country to Visit in 2025, a development hailed as a resounding affirmation of the nation’s tourism revival and growing international confidence in Brand Zimbabwe. On the other hand, Tourism and Hospitality Industry Minister Barbara Rwodzi was named Africa's Tourism Minister of the Year at ITB Berlin 2026, reflecting growing global confidence in the country's tourism sector. The growing international recognition has significantly boosted Zimbabwe's destination brand, reinforcing its competitiveness in the global tourism marketplace and providing stakeholders with a strong platform to attract more visitors, investment and tourism business. The National Tourism Sector Strategy (2026-2030) is expected to provide a roadmap for accelerating tourism growth, enhancing competitiveness and increasing the sector's contribution to economic development. The consultations brought together Government, the private sector, interest groups and other tourism stakeholders to develop a roadmap that will guide the industry's growth over the next five years, anchored on improved air connectivity, regional integration, destination competitiveness and sustainable tourism development.

more.title: NECs called on to build strong foundation by embracing innovation
more.description: Judith Phiri judith.phiri@sundaynews.co.zw THE Government has called on National Employment Councils (NECs) to build a strong foundation by embracing innovation, strengthening institutional capacity and positioning themselves as strategic partners in national development. Zimbabwe has 48 NECs which are statutory, bipartite bodies that regulate minimum wages, working conditions and dispute resolution within specific industries. In a keynote address and officially opening the 1th Annual Symposium for Chairpersons, Councillors and General Secretaries, Public Service, Labour and Social Welfare Minister Edgar Moyo said as the labour market continues to evolve, NECs must build on a strong foundation by advancing. “Embrace innovation, strengthen institutional capacity and position yourselves as strategic partners in national development. Beyond their traditional responsibilities, NECs should support enterprise competitiveness, promote productivity, facilitate skills development and generate labour market information that contributes to evidence-based policy formulation,” he said. “I am encouraged by the progress already being made by many NECs. The increasing use of digital technologies and social media has improved stakeholder engagement, communication and public awareness of NEC services.” He said these initiatives demonstrate a commitment to ensuring that labour institutions remain accessible and responsive in an increasingly digital environment. The Minister said equally commendable was the expansion of some NECs’ operations through the establishment of provincial and district offices, bringing labour administration services closer to employers and workers throughout the country. “This decentralisation improves access to justice, strengthens compliance and enhances service delivery. It is also pleasing to note that many NECs have also invested in staff development, stakeholder engagement, public awareness programmes and strategic partnerships,” he added. “These initiatives continue to strengthen social dialogue, improve compliance with labour standards and contribute to increased productivity, investor confidence and sustainable economic growth.” He said while they celebrate these achievements, they must also acknowledge that the world of work is undergoing unprecedented transformation. Minister Moyo said the continued expansion of the informal economy, rapid technological advancement, automation, artificial intelligence, digitalisation, climate change and global economic uncertainties are reshaping employment patterns and the skills required for future success. “These developments require labour market institutions that are agile, forward-looking and capable of anticipating change rather than merely responding to it. “NECs must therefore continue to promote innovation, lifelong learning and practical solutions that prepare employers and workers for the future while strengthening Zimbabwe's labour market resilience,” he said. The two day event convened through the partnership between the NECs and the Ministry of Public Service, Labour and Social Welfare is running under the theme, “Enhancing NECs' Resilience and Adaptability in a Dynamic Labour Market Towards Vision 2030.” The symposium provides an important platform to reflect on the future of labour administration, exchange experiences, strengthen partnerships and identify practical solutions that will enable NECs to remain effective, relevant and responsive in a dynamic labour environment. In attendance this year are also a delegation from the Namibia Labour Inspection Exchange Programme.
